narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score and plans to attend law school full-time, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this rejection notice for our program. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on your first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 67% chance that you would not pass, which is the threshold that led to denying your application. This prediction is based on a careful look at key parts of your academic record and background compared to thousands of similar students. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which placed you in the bottom group—much lower than the typical student's mid-range standing—and this had the strongest negative impact overall. Your Year 1 decile, also in the lowest group and below average, added to that concern as the next most influential factor pushing toward a fail prediction. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 36 stood out positively, as it's close to the dataset average of 36.7 and helped nudge the model toward expecting success. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5, however, which is notably below the typical 3.21 average, contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction. Your lower-middle family income bracket and full-time enrollment plans offered small positive boosts compared to peers, but they weren't enough to outweigh the academic concerns. In essence, while your test scores and background show promise, your lower decile rankings in school grades signal higher risk to the model when stacked against students who typically pass the bar. We truly empathize with your situation and see your potential, especially as a dedicated full-time student. To strengthen future applications, we recommend focusing on boosting your GPA and school rankings through targeted academic support or retakes, perhaps while gaining relevant experience like internships. You're not defined by this one prediction—many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these areas head-on, and we encourage you to reapply after showing improvement. Keep pushing forward; your story is still being written.
